reviews.author: Measured Merit
reviews.text: Beautiful place of residence and fascinating mausoleum .... A captivating spot for people-watching, blending international visitors with stunning local models ahead of Tet celebrations. The expansive grounds feature gorgeous flowering trees encircling a surprisingly large lake, creating an unforgettable romantic haven. Highlights - Vibrant mix of global travelers and elegant locals. - Lush, blooming landscapes around a serene lake. - Perfect for romantic outings or festive ambiance.

reviews.author: Ron Scozzafava
reviews.text: The Mausoleum of Emperor Tu Duc is one of the most serene and thoughtfully designed sites we visited in Huế. Set among pine trees, lakes, and quiet courtyards, it feels less like a tomb and more like a peaceful retreat—which fits well with what we learned about Emperor Tu Duc himself. The layout is elegant and balanced, blending architecture with nature in a way that encourages you to slow down and take it all in. Walking through the pavilions, temples, and gardens gives real insight into imperial life, philosophy, and the emperor’s reflective personality. It’s calm, unhurried, and surprisingly intimate. As with many historical sites in Huế, having context makes a big difference. A good guide—or a solid guidebook—adds depth and helps connect the design choices to the emperor’s life and the broader history of the Nguyen dynasty. Overall, this is a beautiful and contemplative place, well worth visiting. Less grand in scale than the Imperial City, but emotionally richer and more personal.

reviews.author: Erik Blaney
reviews.text: Stunning park like setting where the emperor used to come to as an escape from the forbidden city. Although the shrine is located on site no one actually knows where his body is. Lunar New Year was an excellent time to come as all the young kids and teens were dressed up in traditional clothing taking pictures throughout the monument

reviews.author: Linh Vu
reviews.text: Emperor Tu Duc tomb is a beautiful place to visit. It is a well-landscaped area where you get a glimpse of the beautiful gardens of the past where the king and his family enjoyed strolling in nature and feeding the fish at the koi pond. Great place to learn about the past and take pictures. Best to come dressed in Vietnamese ao dai or rent one on site.

reviews.author: Delia Walsh
reviews.text: Personally I preferred this over the other royal tombs in the area. The site was much larger than I thought it would be, and was lovely to walk around. The grounds were serine and very well kept. Not many information plaques that I could find so maybe an audioguide would be good. Lots of trees so plenty of opportunity to stay in the shade on sunny days.

reviews.author: Nikolaus Mairböck
reviews.text: Some of the main Buildings are blocked off because of renovation going on, but there is still plenty to see if you walk past and around the park. Compared to the entry fee at the citadel its a bit pricey, but there are also combo tickets available if you want to visit the citadel and at least 2 of the tombs. The park a d fish ponds are very quiet, its a good place to get away from the noise of the city

reviews.author: Reginald Williams
reviews.text: Stunning beyond my wildest expectations. I could have spent hours there other than for the heat. Without question the visit here has been my highlight of the whole trip to Hue. Early, early morning or very late afternoon most highly recommended.

reviews.author: TatyanKA G
reviews.text: This isn't the most popular of the tombs, but it's the most interesting. There's a large park here, where you can stroll in the shade or sit by the lake. Plus, it's close to the city, so it's easy to just wander around in the pleasant atmosphere.

reviews.author: John Winter
reviews.text: This was by far my favorite mausoleum in Hue. It is set within stunning gardens filled with tall pine trees, peaceful ponds, and traditional Vietnamese architecture. It’s a beautifully laid-out complex with lots of open space to walk around, relax, and take in the views. The area is well maintained and shows great care in its restoration efforts. Everything from the stone paths to the wooden pavilions feels thoughtfully preserved. The large lake in the center reflects the buildings and trees beautifully, adding to the tranquil atmosphere. We spent about 45 minutes here, but you could easily stay longer if you enjoy photography or just want to soak up the peaceful surroundings. It wasn’t too crowded when we went, which made it even more enjoyable. Highly recommend this one if you’re visiting Hue—especially if you’re only picking one or two mausoleums to explore. It combines natural beauty, history, and serenity all in one place. Tuesday 7 am–5:30 pm Wednesday. 7 am–5:30 pm Thursday 7 am–5:30 pm Friday 7 am–5:30 pm Saturday 7 am–5:30 pm Sunday 7 am–5:30 pm Monday 7 am–5:30 pm

reviews.author: Han Nguyen
reviews.text: The resting place of Tu Duc King, the longest-reigning emperor of the Nguyen Dynasty. This mausoleum is one of the four most beautiful mausoleums in Hue, famed for its poetic and elegant architecture. This enormous construction has 2 main parts: palace and tomb. Take time too walk around and explore this place!

reviews.author: Philip Blair
reviews.text: Seen on an extremely wet day (serious flooding in Hue city) this is an interesting complex of temples, buildings and artifacts set in 12 hectares of beautifully laid out grounds with attractive lake, bridges and a temple on stilts. Worth a visit.

reviews.author: S. Egberink
reviews.text: Tranquil sight to visit away from buzzing center of Hue. Sufficient information given at the sight. Worth the combo-ticket. Back in time to a world so near and yet so far.

reviews.author: J G
reviews.text: More extensive than some of the other tombs, I found this one really interesting and beautiful with tombs for family members of the Emperor as well, some areas very slippery when wet so take extra care

reviews.author: Erpel
reviews.text: Nice place Very big, take your time. Buildings in good condition and restored. Fishfood for 5k

reviews.author: Vera Bakirova
reviews.text: It’s more like a very serene and beautiful park, you can walk there, think and calm down

reviews.author: Anthony Giarratano
reviews.text: I visited all three of the popular tombs in one day: Khai Dinh, Minh Mang and then Tu Duc. While each was nice in it's own way, this one has the best layout and well-maintained grounds. This was was also the more touristy one. Khai Dinh was the smallest area, built into the side of a hilltop. Minh Mang was the least touristy when I visited.

reviews.author: huy Pham
reviews.text: The Tomb of Tu Duc is a truly serene and beautiful place. It's not just a tomb, but a huge complex of pavilions, temples, and lakes, all set within a peaceful, pine-covered valley. The architecture is stunning, reflecting the emperor's poetic and romantic nature. You can easily spend an hour or two here, walking around the grounds and enjoying the tranquility. It's a must-visit if you're in Huế!

reviews.author: Nam Hồ Phan Hoài
reviews.text: Tomb of Tu Duc is my favorite place to escape the city for a bit. As someone from Hue, I’ve been there many times, yet every visit feels peaceful and new. The space is open and full of greenery, with calm lakes and ancient architecture that feels like stepping into an old poem. If you’re visiting Hue, don’t miss Tu Duc Tomb. It’s beautiful, tranquil, and rich with imperial charm — perfect for a quiet, soulful afternoon.

reviews.author: tahra brady
reviews.text: This was our favourite Mausoleum. So peaceful and beautiful. So much to see and could spend a while just wandering around and sitting by the pond.

reviews.author: Arlene Lum
reviews.text: Amazing Tomb of Tu Duc! This attraction was so wonderful, from the gorgeous grounds to wander around to the grand tombs and memorials, everything here seemed ready for an amazing photograph. The memorials were grand with many steps up to impressive gates, towering columns, and the forests were so peaceful. There was grass under the trees despite the shade, which made it feel lush and relaxing. The grounds were so enjoyable to wander around and so well kept, you can imagine what a fine place this was for an emperor, just beautiful. Highly recommended if you are in Hue.

reviews.author: Sergio Merchan
reviews.text: A spacious mausoleum with lakes, pavilions, and tree-lined paths. Its style is more classic and modest compared to others, but it offers beautiful corners to walk around. We expected it to be quieter, but there were quite a few people when we visited. Still, it’s worth seeing for its historical value and peaceful setting.

reviews.author: Rich Meska
reviews.text: A must see. I would recommend you have a guide or a great travel book to learn the history here.

reviews.author: Adam Herrera
reviews.text: This is a great place worth visiting. There is food and drinks inside, English signs describing each location and its significence. There is also audio and paid tours available at a low cost. You should also understand that there is a lot of walking involved and not wheelchair or stroller friendly. Be prepared to go up lots of slippery stairs. Obviously during this time period they didnt think about this stuff. 😅

reviews.author: Jimmy Farrow
reviews.text: The grounds of this Mausoleum are beautiful. It's a nice place to take a stroll or get some lovely Insta photos. However, the heritage part and some of the buildings were under heavy construction, which killed its vibe when I visited. Also, when you park your motorbike, a few older ladies will pop out of their store and say free parking, but will pester you to buy a coconut when you come out. You are not obliged to but just a pre warning.

reviews.author: Do Cuong
reviews.text: This is the final resting place of Emperor Tu Duc, who had the longest reign of any monarch in the Nguyen dynasty, from 1848 to 1883. Even though he had over a hundred wives and concubines, he couldn't have a son. So, he had to write his own epitaph, which he thought was a bad omen. But you can still see that epitaph on a huge stone tablet just east of his tomb. This stone is the biggest of its kind in Vietnam, and it took four years to get it there from a quarry more than 500 kilometers away. Tu Duc started planning his tomb way before he died in 1883. The main parts were finished between 1864 and 1867, and he actually used the buildings here as his royal retreat for the rest of his life. The construction demanded so much labor and extra taxes that it even caused a failed rebellion in 1866. The amenities here for the living are unmatched at any other tomb in Vietnam. The emperor could go boating on the lake or hunt small animals on the tiny island in the middle. He would hang out at the Xung Khiem Pavilion and write poetry with his concubines. After their boat trips, they’d moor at the Du Khiem Pavilion and walk right into the main palace area of the tomb. After the emperor died in 1883, his adopted son Kien Phuc took over but only ruled for seven months before he passed away. So instead of his own tomb, he was buried in a small corner of Tu Duc’s tomb grounds. The tomb of Empress Le Thien Anh, Tu Duc's main wife, is located between the two.

reviews.author: Kypu Dương
reviews.text: Tự Đức Tomb is one of the most beautiful and poetic royal tombs in Huế. Surrounded by pine trees, lotus-filled lakes, and quiet stone paths, the site feels more like a peaceful garden retreat than a royal burial ground. Built by Emperor Tự Đức during his lifetime, the tomb reflects his love for poetry, nature, and art. The architecture is elegant and harmonious, blending seamlessly with the landscape — a true reflection of Vietnamese aesthetics and imperial philosophy. It’s the perfect place for a calm, reflective walk, and a must-visit if you want to experience the quieter, more contemplative side of Huế’s imperial history.

reviews.author: jr t
reviews.text: He was emperor from 1848-1883. The site is far bigger that the Minh Mang tomb, some area are under restoration. You will be able to see the tomb of the emperor, his wife and his adoptive son.

reviews.author: Fabio Würz
reviews.text: Beautiful area outside of hue. Tombs, buildings, ruins, a big park, everything combined.

reviews.author: B W
reviews.text: Pretty spectacular but recommend coming in early morning or late afternoon in hot season. Would be best in the rainy cold season - more atmospheric. 200,000 entry is steep.

reviews.author: KaramjaHead
reviews.text: An incredibly beautiful historical site to visit, perfect for learning about the history and the war that took place there. It can get quite busy, but the area is vast, and many parts are being reconstructed to resemble old buildings from the past. You might also come across friendly students, and if you're lucky, you could take a photo with them.
